text: ni Magidiɛl ni Iram. Bɛmbɔŋɔ n-nyɛ Ɛdom nanima; di gbinni nyɛla Iso ŋun nyɛ Ɛdomnim’ yab’ la kaman bɛ biɛhigu shee ni daa nyɛ luɣ’ shɛŋa bɛ ni daa su tiŋgbɔn shɛli maa ni.
translated: and Magdiel and Iram. These were the chiefs of Edom; that is, according to their settlements in the land they possessed. Esau was the father of the Edomites.

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Ka o biɛyanim’ maa bɔh’ o, “A shiri nyɛla ŋun ti yɛn nyɛ ti naa? Bee a shiri nyɛla ŋun ti yɛn su ti fukumsi?” Dinzuɣu ka bɛ jɛlinsi zaŋ chaŋ o sani daa maan pahi, o ni daa zahim zahin’ shɛŋa maa min’ o yɛtɔɣa maa zuɣu.
translated: And his parents asked him, “Is this the child who you say was born blind? How then does he now see?” They asked because they were afraid of the leaders, since the leaders had already agreed that anyone who said he was the Christ would be put out of the synagogue.

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Ka o daa yɛl’ o, “Pumpɔŋɔ, cham’ ti nya a biɛyanim’ maa mini biŋkɔbiri maa mal’ alaafee, ka labina ti yɛli ma.” Di saha ka o daa tim o ka o yi Hiburɔn vinvamli ni chaŋ Shɛkɛm.
translated: And he said to him, "Please, let me go and see if my brothers and the flock are well, and bring me word." So he sent him from the Valley of Hebron, and he went to Shechem.

ref: and wandering through the fields, when a man asked, “What are you looking for?”
text: Ka do’ so daa ti nya o ka o be mɔɣu ni chani ginda, ka o bɔh’ o, “Bɔ ka a lee bɔra?”
translated: When his father heard what he had done and saw the money, he asked him, “Where did you get this from?”

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: Rubin daa yɛli ba, “Di yihiya o ʒim, zaŋmiy’ o niŋ bɔɣili ŋɔ ni mɔɣu ŋɔ ni, ka di niŋy’ o shɛli.” O daa yɛlila lala ni o fa o bɛ nuu ni bahi n-zaŋ o kulisi ti ti bɛ ba.
translated: Rubin said to them, “Don’t shed blood, throw him into this well here in the wilderness, but don’t lay a hand on him.” He said this so that he could rescue him from them and take him back to his father.

ref: Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him. After all, he is our brother.” And the others agreed.
text: Chɛliya ka ti zaŋ o kɔhi Ishimail zuliya ŋɔ zuɣu, ka di chɛliya ka ti niŋ o shɛli, dama o nyɛla ti tizo ni timaŋmaŋ’ niŋgbuŋ.” Ka o mabihi maa daa wum o ni yɛli shɛm maa.
translated: The angel said, “Go and take Ishmael’s mother by the hand, and the angel said, ‘I will make him a great nation.’” And his mother was very happy when she heard this.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ka Midiannim’ maa daa zaŋ Yisifu chaŋ Ijipti nti kɔhi Pɔtifa ŋun daa nyɛ tɔbbi’ shɛb’ bɛn guli Firawuna la sapashin’ kpɛm’ zuɣu.
translated: When the Midianites took Joseph to Egypt, they sold him to Potiphar, who was one of Pharaoh’s officials and the captain of the guard.

ref: Onan knew the child would not be his, and when he had sex with Tamar, he made sure that she would not get pregnant.
text: Amaa ka Onan daa mi ni o bi yɛn su bihi maa. Dinzuɣu o min’ o biɛli pakoli maa daa yi ti dɔni, o daa yihirila o kom bahi tiŋa ni di chɛ ka o ku dɔɣi bihi ti o biɛli maa.
translated: But Onan did not want to have children for his brother. Whenever he slept with his brother’s wife, he spilled his semen on the ground to avoid giving offspring to his brother.

ref: The Lord wasn't pleased with Onan and took his life too.
text: Ka o ni daa niŋ shɛm maa bi niŋ Yawɛ nyaɣisim, ka o daa ku o gba.
translated: If he had listened to his mother's advice, he would not have died.

ref: Judah did not want the same thing to happen to his son Shelah, and he told Tamar, “Go home to your father and live there as a widow until my son Shelah is grown.” So Tamar went to live with her father.
text: Di saha ka Juda daa yɛl’ o bia pakoli Tama, “Kulim’ a ba yiŋ’ ti ʒini pakolim hal ka m bia Shɛla ti zooi.” O daa zɔrimi ni Shɛla gba ti kpi kaman o biɛyanim’ maa ni daa kpi shɛm maa. Dinzuɣu ka Tama daa kul’ o ba yiŋ’ ti ʒiya.
translated: It happened that Judah said to his daughter-in-law Tamar, “Stay as a widow in your father’s house until my son Shelah grows up.” He said this because he thought Shelah might die like his brothers. So Tamar went and stayed in her father’s house.

ref: “What do you want?” he asked in return. “The ring on that cord around your neck,” was her reply. “I also want the special walking stick you have with you.” He gave them to her, they slept together, and she became pregnant.
text: Ka Juda bɔh’ o, “Bɔ ka a lee bɔri ni n zaŋ ti a ka di nyɛ alikauli lɔbu shɛhira?” Ka paɣ’ maa garigi yɛl’ o, “Zaŋm’ a nintua din mali dalinli ŋɔ mini zoligo ŋɔ ni a jaaŋgbee din be a nuu ni ŋɔ ti ma.” Di saha ka o daa zaŋ li ti o, ka zaŋ o dɔni, ka o daa niŋ pua.
translated: When Judas asked him, “Why wasn’t this perfume sold and the money given to the poor?” But the woman replied, “Leave her alone so that she may keep it for the day of my burial.” After that, he left and went away, and he did not return.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.
text: Ka o dan’ maa daa nya ka Yawɛ be Yisifu sani, ka chɛ ka o ni niŋdi shɛlikam zaa tɔŋda.
translated: When his father heard what Yahweh had done for Joseph, he was completely filled with joy.

ref: No one in my master's house is more important than I am. The only thing he hasn't given me is you, and that's because you are his wife. I won't sin against God by doing such a terrible thing as this.”
text: O bi galisi gari ma yili ŋɔ puuni, o mi bi mɔŋ ma shɛli naɣila nyini, dama a nyɛla o paɣa; ka wula ka n ni tooi tum ninvuɣ’ biɛtali tuun’ titali ŋɔ n-tum alahichi zaŋ chaŋ Naawuni sani?”
translated: He/she has been living in that house for a long time, but I have never seen his/her husband, so I thought she was single; but now that I see children playing around, does it mean God has blessed her with children?

ref: She kept begging Joseph day after day, but he refused to do what she wanted or even to go near her.
text: Hal o ni daa kul yɛri Yisifu lala dabisili kam maa zaa yoli, Yisifu daa bi saɣi ni o min’ o dɔni.
translated: When his brothers saw Joseph coming from afar, they plotted against him, but Joseph did not know their intentions.

ref: Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's coat until her husband came home.
text: Di saha ka o daa zaŋ Yisifu binyɛrigu maa sɔŋ baɣil’ omaŋ’ hal ka Yisifu dan’ maa ti kuna.
translated: They agreed and sold Joseph to the traders who took him away, and Joseph did not return home again.

ref: I held the king's cup and squeezed the grapes into it, then I gave the cup to the king.”
text: Ka Firawuna pipia daa be n nuu ni, ka m pɔhi wain tiwala maa kahim di kom niŋ Firawuna pipia maa ni n-zaŋ li ti ti ŋun’ Firawuna.”
translated: When Pharaoh’s daughter came and saw the child, she felt sorry for him and said, “This is one of the Hebrew children.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Ka Yisifu daa garigi yɛl’ o, “Di gbinni m-bɔŋɔ: Piɛr’ ata maa nyɛla daba ata.
translated: When Yisifu was about to die, he said, "Listen to me carefully: Peter is not the same as the other man."

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: Zaŋ chaŋ daba ata dali, Firawuna ni chɛ ka bɛ kana ti yih’ a ti ŋma a zuɣu, ka zaŋ a yili tia ka noonsi di a nimdi.”
translated: Three days later, Pharaoh said that they should not let us go, so he gathered the elders and discussed the matter.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: amaa ka daa chɛ ka bɛ zaŋ o bɔrɔbɔro shɛriba kpɛm’ maa ti yil’ tia kaman Yisifu ni daa yɛli ba shɛm maa.
translated: But every time they went to his house, they found the door locked because Yusuf’s mother did not want them to disturb her.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Yuma ayi nyaaŋa ka Firawuna daa ti zahim zahindi n-nya ka o ʒiɛla Nail Mɔɣili duli.
translated: God did not punish Pharaoh because he did not know, but because he crossed the River Nile arrogantly.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Ka kawaan’ kɔri maa daa zaŋ kawaan’ kara ayopɔin maa vali. Ka Firawuna daa yirigi neei n-nya ka di nyɛla zahindi.
translated: And the cow was standing by the river while other cows were coming up out of the river. And Pharaoh woke up from his sleep and realized it was a dream.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Ka di daa muɣis’ o pam asibaasi, ka o tim ti boli Ijipti timanim’ mini baŋdiba zaa na, ka ŋun’ Firawuna daa yɛli ba o zahima maa, amaa ka so daa kani n-nyɛ ŋun ni tooi wuhi Firawuna o zahima maa gbinni.
translated: When he led them out of Egypt, he did not take them through the land of the Philistines, even though that was shorter, because God said, “If they face war, they might change their minds and return to Egypt.”

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Di saha ka Firawuna daa tim ka bɛ chaŋ ti boli Yisifu n-zaŋ o sarika maa ni na yomyom; ka o daa pini o ninni ka taɣi binyɛra ye n-kpe Firawuna sani.
translated: So it happened that when Pharaoh died, they went to bury Joseph with great honor; and he fulfilled his promise and did not forget what he had told Pharaoh before.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: ka naɣ’ kara ayopɔin bɛn niŋgbuna kul nyɛlisiri daa yi Nail Mɔɣili maa ni na ti ŋubiri mɔri,
translated: If you do not have a passport, you cannot travel by air to Accra or any other foreign country.

ref: Then seven other heads of grain came up. They were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: ka kawaan’ kɔr’ ayopɔin din nyɛ biisim ni kpe shɛli ka di kul zaɣizaɣimi to n-yina pahi di zuɣu,
translated: If a monkey climbs a tree and does not see anything, it should come down and continue its journey.

ref: The seven skinny, ugly cows that came up later also stand for seven years, as do the seven bad heads of grain that were scorched by the desert wind. The dreams mean there will be seven years when there won't be enough grain.
text: Naɣ’ kɔri bɛba ayopɔin bɛn daa yina n-kul gaŋsigaŋsi la gbinni nyɛla yuma ayopɔin, ka kawaan’ kɔr’ ayopɔin din daa nyɛ biisim ni kpe shɛl’ la gba gbinni nyɛla kum lubu yuma ayopɔin.
translated: If someone wants to open a door and does not have the key, they should not break the lock to open the door, but rather look for the key or wait for the owner to come and open the door.

ref: It is just as I said—God has shown what he intends to do.
text: Di nyɛla kaman n ni pun yɛli nyin’ Firawuna shɛm la, Naawuni n-kahig’ o ni yɛn niŋ shɛli wuh’ a.
translated: Just as you have heard the story of Pharaoh, God destroyed him and those who followed him.

ref: God has given you two dreams to let you know that he has definitely decided to do this and that he will do it soon.
text: Ka nyin’ Firawuna ni nya zahindi maa buyi la wuhiya ni Naawuni n-zal’ li, ka ni chɛ ka di ku yuui, ka di niŋ.
translated: When Pharaoh and his people rejected the truth that had come from God, see what happened to them and how they ended up.

ref: It can be stored until it is needed during the seven years when there won't be enough grain in Egypt. This will keep the country from being destroyed because of the lack of food. Joseph Is Made Governor over Egypt
text: Ka lala bindira maa ni nyɛ a tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ ni bindira n-ʒiɛya gu yuma ayopɔin shɛŋa ni kum ni ti yɛn lu Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ ni maa, ka di chɛ ka kum ti ku ku tiŋgbɔŋ ŋɔ nima.” Bɛ zaŋ Yisifu leei Ijipti gomna
translated: When their father died, they said, “What if Joseph holds a grudge against us and pays us back for all the wrongs we did to him?” So Joseph remained the governor of Egypt.

ref: So the king said to them, “Who could possibly handle this better than Joseph? After all, the Spirit of God is with him.”
text: Ka Firawuna daa bɔh’ o kpambaliba maa, “Ti ni tooi nya ninvuɣ’ so ka o be kaman Naawuni Shia ni be do’ so ni ŋɔ?”
translated: When Pharaoh asked his officials, “Do you know of any god besides Allah that I should worship?”

ref: I'm putting you in charge of my palace, and everybody will have to obey you. No one will be over you except me.
text: Nyini n-yɛn su n yili ŋɔ fukumsi, ka a yi kul yɛli shɛlikam, n niriba zaa ni niŋ li; kulla n nam ʒiishee ŋɔ ka n yɛn zaŋ gar’ a.”
translated: If you wake up in the morning and greet everyone in the house with a smile, all the people there will be happy; even if you have nothing, you will still be respected.

ref: He gave Joseph the Egyptian name Zaphenath Paneah. And he let him marry Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, a priest in the city of Heliopolis. Joseph traveled all over Egypt.
text: Ka Firawuna daa boli Yisifu yuli Zafɛnatipania, ka zaŋ Pɔtifɛra ŋun daa nyɛ Hɛliopolis tindan’ la bipuɣiŋga Asɛnat n-ti Yisifu ni o niŋ o paɣa. Ka Yisifu daa yiri chani gindi Ijipti tiŋgbɔŋ maa.
translated: When Pharaoh named Joseph Zaphenath-Paneah, he gave him Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, priest of Heliopolis, as his wife. And Joseph took charge over the whole land of Egypt.
